danger_signals.visual_form: Large enemy sprites (e.g., Tyrannosaurus in level 1)
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat requiring tactical weapon selection and health management

danger_signals.visual_form: Rival caveman sprites distinct from player characters
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile faction opposition; primary antagonist presence

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental obstacles and dinosaur enemies throughout levels
danger_signals.meaning: World danger from fauna and terrain; health drain mechanic signals ongoing peril

danger_signals.visual_form: Health system depletes over time during regular gameplay
danger_signals.meaning: Persistent world threat; player must maintain momentum or perish

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items (boomerangs, bones, fire, flints, electricity, stone wheels, clubs)
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary power increase; temporary safety through offensive capability

safety_signals.visual_form: Level transition after boss falls; route selection nodes
safety_signals.meaning: Sanctuary/progression moment; player agency restored through level branching

safety_signals.visual_form: Co-op gameplay with both Joe (green-haired) and Mac (blue-haired)
safety_signals.meaning: Collective strength; shared survival burden; mutual support against rival tribe

faction_indicators.color_identity: Joe: green hair; Mac: blue hair
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Rival tribe cavemen (distinct sprite design from Joe/Mac)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Huts as shelter/narrative anchors (Japanese version shows cavemen emerging from hut with kidnapped women)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Enemy caveman sprite variants; kidnapper identity
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposing cavemen as primary humanoid antagonists throughout levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Kidnapped cavewomen as visual MacGuffin and motivation; represents territorial/resource conflict

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple dinosaur types as environmental faction/hazard
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dinosaurs populate levels as primal world state; neutral danger representing nature itself

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level progression toward rival tribe strongholds; women captives as visual narrative element
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy cavemen actively patrol and attack; boss battles escalate in difficulty; health drain pressure increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stone age aesthetics; cave/jungle environments; bone and stone weapon designs; colorful but flat backgrounds
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Weapon variety reflects primitive craftsmanship (boomerangs, flints, stone wheels); no advanced technology presence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Route selection after boss defeats represents tribal domain conquest or avoidance; multiple endings based on player path choices
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player chooses between three exits post-final-boss, indicating different tribal outcome scenarios; branching narrative reflects political choice

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Continuous health decay during normal gameplay (distinct from boss battles where health stable)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Constant forward momentum required; time pressure creates sense of environmental hostility

environmental_storytelling.example: Cavewomen serve as both narrative motivation and visual goal state; Japanese version features hut scene showing forced abduction (removed in US versions for content sensitivity)
Tyrannosaurus in level 1 is visually massive compared to player sprite; conveys prehistoric power hierarchy and danger escalation
Bright colors and comic-style art distinguish this prehistoric adventure as fantastical/stylized rather than realistic; 'cutesie' tone creates contrast with kidnapping narrative
Route selection mechanics allow player to choose which tribal domain to assault next, creating player agency in political conflict progression
Different weapon types (boomerangs, bones, fire, electricity, stone wheels) suggest tribal crafting diversity and adaptation within stone-age constraints

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can jump, run, attack; health depletes over time; can damage enemies or rival player in co-op
Enhanced attack power temporarily available; tactical delay for power-up windows
Active hostility; attack on player proximity; represent constant tribal threat
Health stable (no passive decay); requires specific weapon selection and dodge patterns; defeat triggers route selection and progression
Visual presence only; no interactive behavior; rescue narrative drive
Assists player in combat; can damage rival player in some versions, adding PvP risk to cooperative mode

notes: Joe & Mac communicates world-state primarily through aesthetic (prehistoric, colorful, 'cutesie' comic style) rather than grim realism, creating tonal contrast with the serious kidnapping narrative. The health decay mechanic is a key world-state signal—constant time pressure outside boss encounters conveys environmental hostility. Faction identity is communicated through hair color (protagonist pairing), distinct enemy sprite designs (rival tribe), and environmental presence (dinosaurs). The branching route system is notable: it's both mechanical progression and political narrative, allowing players to choose which tribal domain to engage. The 1992 Genesis port preserves arcade mechanics closely, maintaining these visual communication systems. Content sensitivity differences between Japanese and US versions (hut abduction scene removal) demonstrate how visual presentation of the tribal conflict narrative required cultural adaptation.
